You have rights even during an involuntary hold. Don't hesitate to advocate for yourself and ask questions about your care.

Under California's Welfare & Institutions Code §5332, you may refuse non-emergent medications even while on a 72-hour involuntary psychiatric hold (5150). However, if two physicians independently document that you pose an **imminent danger to yourself or others** (e.g., active suicidal/homicidal intent, severe agitation risking harm), emergency medications may be administered without your consent to ensure safety.

If you do not wish to take the medication, you can express your refusal to the treatment team. They are required to evaluate whether the medication qualifies as "emergency" treatment under the law. You have the right to ask for clarification from your healthcare providers about the rationale for the medication and whether it falls under this exception. Always document your concerns in your medical record if possible.

3. Adjust subsequent medications (e.g., using phenylephrine instead of epinephrine if sulfite allergy is confirmed and further vasopressors are needed).
